intro: The clearest difference between Crowdin and Linear is lock-in — Crowdin has medium lock-in (SaaS localization, translations exportable), whereas Linear has low lock-in (Issue tracker, data exportable). Beyond that, developer experience — Crowdin scores 4/5 against Linear's 5/5. Crowdin fits user onboarding automation; Linear fits SaaS and e-commerce teams managing user journeys. On the strengths side, Crowdin is cited for user onboarding automation, while Linear is cited for centralizes customer data and automates lifecycle messaging. The honest trade-off: Crowdin is the wrong call for pre-launch projects; Linear is the wrong call for single-product apps with no ongoing user engagement; Crowdin users flag pre-launch products, and Linear users flag contact-based pricing scales steeply with list growth. Choosing between them should come down to the constraint that actually binds your workflow, not surface-level overlap. On developer experience the data shows Crowdin at 4/5 and Linear at 5/5, with transparency at 4/5 and 4/5 respectively.
quick_take: Crowdin is for user onboarding automation; Linear is for SaaS and e-commerce teams managing user; decide based on lock-in tolerance.
verdict_a: Choose Crowdin if your use case is user onboarding automation, and medium lock-in is acceptable in exchange for its fit.
verdict_b: Choose Linear if your use case is SaaS and e-commerce teams managing user journeys, and low lock-in is acceptable in exchange for its fit.

faq.a: Both Crowdin and Linear use a freemium model with a free tier, so raw pricing is close on paper. The real cost difference depends on volume, seats, and which paid features you actually need. Check current pricing on each vendor's page before deciding.

faq.q: Can I migrate from Crowdin to Linear?
faq.a: Migrating from Crowdin to Linear is feasible when their scopes overlap, but the work is in re-mapping configuration, data models, and integrations. Moving off Crowdin is harder because of medium lock-in (SaaS localization, translations exportable). Plan for a dual-run period so you can validate parity before decommissioning.

On our developer-experience score, Linear is rated 5/5 and Crowdin 4/5, so Linear edges ahead. Scores like this smooth over real differences in docs, CLI feel, and SDK quality, so treat them as a starting point. Spend an afternoon in each before relying on the number.

faq.q: Is Linear a good alternative to Crowdin?
faq.a: Linear is a reasonable alternative to Crowdin when Linear fits SaaS and e-commerce teams managing user journeys. If your use case matches Crowdin's sweet spot — user onboarding automation — staying with Crowdin is probably safer. Crowdin itself is not meant for pre-launch projects, which is often where users start shopping for alternatives.
